verbatim_text: So what's funny about a subjective experience is it's something hypothetical, not real. Not that it's made of qualia and in a theatre, at least that's the view I'm trying to persuade you to believe in. And I got ahead of my slides here 'cause I got so carried away with this. So it's just an indirect way of me telling you about what's going on in my brain. Obviously, if I told you, "Neuron 52 is firing," that wouldn't do you any good, 'cause in you it will be neuron 57. And anyway, I don't know neuron 52 is firing. That's a lousy way for me to tell you what's going on in my brain. The only way I can tell you what's going on in my brain is by talking about either the normal things that would've caused it, these little hypothetical pink elephants, or I could tell you about what's going on in my brain by telling you about the normal things it would cause. So I can say, if you ask me how I'm feeling, I can say, "I feel like punching Gary on the nose.". So feelings are all about describing what's going on in your brain by talking about hypothetical actions, and percepts, or subjective experiences, are talking about hypothetical inputs.
